What errors would you look for? And how would you keep your process consistent across many lessons? So when I am actually looking at any lesson, you know, we have a curriculum and our lessons are given to us. And of course, the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety, making sure that the objectives align with the standards that the standards are aligned. Um, as an English teacher of 29 years, some of the errors that we look for, we have a tendency to look for spelling and accuracy errors. And we find that a lot. I feel like I find that a lot in some of the curriculum that we have. It's a new curriculum. So we're all often going back as teachers and correcting that to keep it consistent. We have checklists. I have a checklist for almost everything that I do, literally a list for everything I do. Um, so that would keep me consistent with making sure that the lessons continue to be the same throughout the whole entire unit. Okay, so the second question is tell us about your experience catching content or quality errors and education and digital materials. Um, and then give us an example. So again, I have been a teacher for 29 years in English. And so I think it almost comes by nature to look for spelling and grammar and accuracy. Um, back to this curriculum that we just got this year. And I feel like it has kind of been pieced together. Uh, and so we have, I say, we, my team and myself have put to, um, you know, really have to get into it and try to catch. Content errors. I, for this one in particular, I noticed that the assessments didn't. They didn't align with what we were actually teaching. And so it was often that we had to go back and correct those assessments to make sure that, um, that we wanted to assess what it is that we actually taught for that day or in that specific unit. Um, I've also worked for a company where I was a content director, a content developer. Um, I'm a subject matter expert. And so those things also help me go back to catch. Uh, content that. Again, isn't is an accurate, um, in spelling, grammar, consistency. You name it. Uh, when you find an issue, some you can fix yourself and some need to go to developers as a bug ticket.
